content_html:
• A powerful voice from heaven tells John to write again (see Revelation 1:11, 19; 21:5; compare 10:4).

  • Blessed are … those who die in the Lord: God wants those who suffer persecution to be with him and enjoy his blessings and rest.

  • Mentioning hard work and good deeds with eternal reward hints at rewards given based on actions (Revelation 20:12).

title: Revelation 14:14
content_html:
• the Son of Man: Or like a son of man; see study note on Revelation 1:13.

  • A gold crown symbolizes status or power, clearly distinguishing Jesus from the angels (see Revelation 4:4; 6:2; 9:7; 12:3; 13:1).

  • The sharp sickle symbolizes God's harvest of judgment (see Revelation 14:17–18; see also Jeremiah 51:33; Hosea 6:11; Micah 4:12–13).

title: Revelation 14:14–20
content_html: Two short scenes show God's judgment.

title: Revelation 14:15–18
content_html: The temple and the altar symbolize God's presence (see Revelation 9:13; 11:19; 16:7).

title: Revelation 14:18–20
content_html:
• The ripe grapes (see Amos 8:2) trampled in the winepress highlight God's power to judge (see Revelation 19:15; Joel 3:13).

  • People built winepresses outside cities and towns; here, the city is Jerusalem. The bloody river of death stretched about 290 kilometers (180 miles). This shows that God’s enemies have no chance when the Son of Man comes in judgment. This destruction is closely linked with the final battle in Revelation 19:17–21. God is just and fair in giving people exactly what they deserve (see Revelation 16:5–7).

title: Revelation 15:1
content_html: This series of seven final plagues (Revelation 15:1–16:21) completes God's punishment of his enemies (see 16:17; Amos 1–2; Romans 1:18–2:16). Revelation revisits the topic of God's punishment later (Revelation 19:15–21).

title: Revelation 15:1–16.21
content_html: The third and final cycle of seven judgments (see study note on Revelation 6:1–16:21) begins with a vision of God's victorious people singing a hymn of praise (Revelation 15:2–4). Then, a scene of the temple appears (Revelation 15:5–8), where angels come out carrying the bowls of God's judgment for the earth (16:1–21).

title: Revelation 15:2
content_html: The glass sea mixed with fire symbolizes victory through testing. Those who defeated the beast (see Revelation 13:1–8, 15–18) hold harps that represent ultimate peace. Their position on the sea of glass (compare Revelation 4:6) symbolizes their endurance through the fire of persecution (compare Psalm 66:12; Isaiah 43:2; Daniel 3:8–30).

title: Revelation 15:3
content_html:
• O Lord God Almighty! (see also Revelation 1:8; 4:8; 11:17; 16:7; 19:6; 21:22): For persecuted Christians, knowing that God is all-powerful brings comfort and security.

  • The fact that God is just and true supports human integrity in a confused, unjust, and dishonest world.

  • God is the supreme, universal King of the nations, not a local deity tied to one nation or a human ruler with limited power.

title: Revelation 15:3–4
content_html: The song of Moses and the Lamb shows that God’s purposes in the old covenant (God’s earlier promise and relationship with his people) and the new covenant (his promise through Jesus) are united and work together.

title: Revelation 15:4
content_html:
• Who will not fear … and glorify Your name?: This rhetorical question (see Jeremiah 10:7) suggests that only a fool would fail to do so (see Revelation 14:7; 16:9; Psalms 14:1; 53:1).

  • You alone are holy: See Revelation 16:5; Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16. God's holiness is the reason for our worship and salvation.

  • All nations will come and worship: Some will be forced to acknowledge God (compare Philippians 2:10–11), but all will recognize that God's deeds and judgments are revealed, righteous, and just.

title: Revelation 15:5–6
content_html:
• God's tabernacle shows God's presence (see John 1:14). Even the plagues come from God's presence. Christ's sacrifice (Matthew 27:51; Mark 15:38; Hebrews 6:19–20; 10:19–20) removed the barrier between God and humanity (see Exodus 26:31–35; Leviticus 16). Now, those who do not belong to Christ feel the full impact of God's presence (see Revelation 20:11–15; Isaiah 13:4–12; Zephaniah 2:1–3; Malachi 3:2–3).

  • bright linen: Linen, a symbol of purity, was also worn by priests during their duties (see Exodus 28:39–43; Leviticus 16:4, 23; see also Ezekiel 9:2–3; Daniel 12:6–7). These angels do not intercede; they are agents of judgment. The linen represents the purity and justice of God's judgment.

  • The gold sashes across their chests likely symbolize their divine mission as ministers of justice for God (compare Revelation 1:13).

title: Revelation 15:7
content_html:
• the four living creatures: See Revelation 4:6–8.

  • seven golden bowls: These bowls are likely similar to the offering pans used in ancient worship (see Exodus 37:16).

title: Revelation 15:8
content_html: Smoke filled the temple to show God's glory and power (Isaiah 6:4; see Exodus 19:16–18; 40:34–35; 1 Kings 8:10–13).

title: Revelation 16:1
content_html: The powerful voice likely belongs to God (also in Revelation 6:6; 9:13; 16:17; 18:4; 19:5; see Isaiah 66:6).

title: Revelation 16:1–21
content_html: These seven bowls filled with God's wrath symbolize the final judgments on the world. With the seventh bowl, a voice from God's throne shouts, It is done! (Revelation 16:17; compare John 19:30).

title: Revelation 16:2
content_html:
• The harmful sores remind us of the sixth plague in Egypt (Exodus 9:9–11).

  • mark of the beast: See Revelation 13:13–17; 14:9–10.

title: Revelation 16:3–4
content_html: The plagues of the second and third bowls are similar to the first plague of Egypt (Exodus 7:17–21). John may have thought about how much of Rome’s food and wealth came from the sea. After Julius Caesar cleared the sea of pirates, shipping became vital for Rome. Its end would mean economic collapse for the empire (see Revelation 18:17–19).

title: Revelation 16:5–6
content_html: the angel of the waters: In Scripture, angels and archangels are shown to have special roles within the order of heaven (see Daniel 8:16; 9:21; 10:13, 21; 12:1; Luke 1:19, 26; see also 1 Enoch 66:1–2). This angel confirms that these judgments come from God, who is just and holy.

title: Revelation 16:6
content_html: Punishing God's enemies is fair because they killed God's holy people and prophets. Since they shed blood, they must drink blood. The principle of lex talionis (the law of retribution), which is the basis of Roman and Jewish law, shows that God is completely fair in judging and rewarding people based on their actions (see Revelation 2:23; 11:18; 14:13; 18:6; 20:12, 13; 22:12).

title: Revelation 16:7
content_html: I heard the altar reply: The response to the angel’s proclamation (Revelation 16:5–6) confirms God’s authority and justice. This praise during judgment reminds persecuted Christians that God cares about his suffering servants and fulfills his purpose in everything.

title: Revelation 16:8–9
content_html:
• The fourth bowl is different from the plagues of Egypt, earlier seals, or trumpets.

  • the people were scorched: Contrast Revelation 7:16. Yet those who faced this judgment refused to repent or honor God (see Revelation 9:20–21; 16:11; compare 14:6–7); they even cursed God's name.

title: Revelation 16:10–11
content_html:
• the throne of the beast: John might be referring to Rome, the political power of his time. Built on seven hills (Revelation 17:9), Rome's empire stretched across the sea (13:1) and dominated the world (13:7).

  • plunged into darkness: This is similar to the ninth plague of Egypt (Exodus 10:22) and the fourth trumpet (Revelation 8:12).

  • gnaw their tongues. … curse the God of heaven: Pain and anger caused these reactions to punishment (compare Matthew 8:12; 13:42, 50; 22:13; 24:51; 25:30; Luke 13:28).

title: Revelation 16:12
content_html: The Euphrates, the largest river in Mesopotamia, lay between Babylon and Israel. It marked the eastern border of the Roman Empire. If it dried up, kings from the east could move their armies westward (compare Revelation 9:13–16). From John's perspective as an ancient Jewish writer, people would always associate these armies with Mesopotamia, not with countries farther east like China or India.

title: Revelation 16:13–14
content_html: Three deceitful agents, evil spirits in the form of frogs, symbolize the demonic role of the evil trinity. Although evil powers fight against the Lord, God the Almighty, not any evil power, remains in control.

title: Revelation 16:15
content_html:
Readers are warned to stay alert because the Lord will come as unexpectedly as a thief (see also Revelation 3:3; Matthew 24:43; 25:13; 1 Thessalonians 5:2; 2 Peter 3:10). Being ready for his coming requires preparation. Those who are not prepared will lack proper clothing and will find themselves exposed (see Matthew 22:11–13). This imagery refers to two failing churches:

  • lifeless Sardis, which is told to “wake up” (Revelation 3:2), and

  • lukewarm Laodicea, which is advised to get proper clothing (Revelation 3:18).

title: Revelation 16:16
content_html:
The name Armageddon (or Harmagedon) likely comes from the Hebrew words har (which means “mountain” or “hill”) and Megiddo. Solomon fortified Megiddo along with Gezer in the south and Hazor in the north (1 Kings 9:15). The fortress of Megiddo stood on a hill in the largest pass through the Carmel range, strategically guarding the Jezreel Valley.

The city was on the Via Maris, the main highway between Egypt and Mesopotamia. Many armies used this route, making the site known for bloody battles. For example, Pharaoh Neco killed Josiah here on his way to fight the Assyrians (2 Kings 23:29). Armageddon became a symbol for the final conflict between God and the forces of evil.

title: Revelation 16:17
content_html: When the seventh bowl is poured out, something unexpected happens. God's enemies gather for battle. But at the crucial moment, instead of a battle, there is a shout: It is done! Jesus spoke these same words from the cross when he completed his work (John 19:30). No one can ultimately fight against God. This scene shows the end of rebellion against God. What follows are different descriptions of the end.

title: Revelation 16:18–20
content_html: This judgment scene repeats the events showing the world's destruction (Revelation 6:12–14; 11:13). It also hints at the final judgment (Revelation 20:11–15) when the earth will be taken apart to allow for a new creation (21:1; compare Isaiah 45:2; Romans 8:19–22).

title: Revelation 16:21
content_html:
• Despite the severity of these plagues, people around the world cursed God instead of understanding his reasons for the judgments (see Revelation 9:20; 16:9, 11).

  • The terrible hailstorm reminds us of the seventh plague on Egypt (Exodus 9:23–24).

title: Revelation 17:1
content_html:
One of the angels who carry out God’s judgment speaks to John and calls him to a new scene. In this scene, John sees the coming judgment of the great prostitute, who rules over many waters.

Rome, which stood on the Tiber River, controlled major centers of power and the water trade routes across the Mediterranean world. Its influence reached from the British Isles to the Euphrates River.

title: Revelation 17:1–19.10
content_html: The main drama in this section shows the powers that stand against God and cause the persecution and suffering of God’s people. Rome’s power attracted and impressed many people (Revelation 17:6). John clearly describes Rome's sins and shares God's judgment (Revelation 17:3–18) before he describes Rome’s fall (18:1–24) and heaven’s response to that fall (19:1–10).

title: Revelation 17:2
content_html:
• immoral with her: This is a biblical image for worshiping other gods (see, for example, Exodus 34:12–16; Judges 2:17; Hosea 2).

  • intoxicated with … her immorality: In Scripture, drunkenness often represents nations that engage in reckless and immoral behavior (see Revelation 18:3, 9; Jeremiah 25:27; 51:7; Lamentations 4:21; Ezekiel 23:33).

title: Revelation 17:3–4
content_html:
• John is taken in the spirit into the wilderness, which he uses to describe a visionary experience (see Revelation 1:10; 4:2). He sees a representation of Rome's moral decay and extreme luxury (see Revelation 18:12–13, 16). This shows how such wealth can be offensive to God.

  • The beast here resembles the second figure in the evil trinity (see Revelation 13:1–4).

  • blasphemous names: See Revelation 13:5–6.

  • purple and scarlet: Clothing in these colors signified royalty and immorality, respectively.

  • a golden cup: See Jeremiah 51:7.

title: Revelation 17:5
content_html: In symbolic language, John likely referred to Rome (see Revelation 17:9) as BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF PROSTITUTES. Babylon symbolized the world's idolatries and evil acts. As the MOTHER, she produced followers who imitated her behavior. Rome, like Babylon, worshipped false gods and led other nations into unfaithfulness and idol worship.

title: Revelation 17:6
content_html: God’s holy people were witnesses for Jesus, even shedding their blood (see Revelation 16:6; Hebrews 12:4). During John's time, Rome caused this persecution.

title: Revelation 17:7
content_html: The angel helps John understand the mystery in response to his amazement (Revelation 17:8–14).

title: Revelation 17:8
content_html:
• The beast … it was, and now is no more, but is about to come up: When Domitian was emperor, from AD 81 to 96, he was as evil as Nero. Both fiercely persecuted the church. Many believed Domitian embodied Nero’s spirit or was Nero himself. This is similar to how people thought John the Baptist embodied Elijah’s spirit (see Malachi 4:5; Matthew 11:14; Luke 1:17; John 1:21).

  • you saw: The past tense shows the angel explained this after John’s vision.

  • it was, and now is no more: This contrasts with God, who is and was and is to come (Revelation 1:4, 8).

  • the Abyss: See study note on Revelation 9:1–12.

  • Unlike God’s people, the people of this world are not in the Book of Life (see Revelation 20:11–15). They will be amazed by the beast’s apparent resurrection (see study note on Revelation 17:10–11; see also 13:13–17).

title: Revelation 17:8–11
content_html: This vision's destruction contrasts with the future of God's people in the new heaven and earth (see Revelation 21:1–4).

title: Revelation 17:9
content_html:
• The seven heads of the beast symbolize the seven hills where Rome was built (refer to study note on Revelation 16:10–11).

  • the woman: See Revelation 17:3–6.

title: Revelation 17:10–11
content_html:
• Five have fallen: If these kings represent Roman emperors, and we start counting from the change from a republic to an empire with Augustus, who ruled from 29 BC to AD 14, then the fifth would be Nero, who ruled from AD 54 to 68, the sixth would be Vespasian, and the seventh would be Titus. The eighth king would be Domitian.

  • that was, and now is not: Early commentators argued that Domitian embodied Nero’s spirit (see study note on Revelation 17:8). 

  • the other seven: This seemingly unbeatable ruler was also destined for destruction.

title: Revelation 17:12–13
content_html: The ten horns, which symbolize world kingdoms following the beast, rule together under his guidance. These verses have led to speculation about a ten-nation alliance. People have guessed it could be the client kingdoms of Rome, the states against the Holy Roman Empire, or the European Union. However, these guesses miss the main point: all nations opposing God will be defeated (Revelation 17:14).

title: Revelation 17:14
content_html: No matter how much power the beast and ten kings gather (Revelation 17:11–12), they cannot win because Jesus is the Lord of all lords (see 19:11–16). Those whom God has called and chosen, and who stay faithful, will stand with him as victors.

title: Revelation 17:15–16
content_html: Although the prostitute controls the masses, she does not win. Instead, the beast hates and kills her. Satan harms even those he uses for evil.

title: Revelation 17:17
content_html: John reminds us that God is in control. God places a plan in the enemy's mind to fulfill his divine purposes (for example, Exodus 7:3; 35:31–35; Ezra 7:27; Romans 9:18, 21).

title: Revelation 18:1
content_html: This angel got his brightness from heaven. The word glory usually describes a divine presence.

title: Revelation 18:1–3
content_html: The angel's powerful taunt song is the first poetic response. Ancient taunt songs mocked a defeated enemy (see Isaiah 13:19–22; 34:10–17). These taunts describe the fall of Babylon (or Rome; see study note on Revelation 14:8) as a traumatic event for dependent kingdoms and especially for those who had benefited from her wealth. The Goths and Visigoths attacked Rome in the AD 400s, ending the prosperous empire.

title: Revelation 18:1–24
content_html: This chapter includes seven poetic responses to the fall of Babylon or Rome (refer to the study note on Revelation 17:5).

title: Revelation 18:2
content_html: Fallen, fallen is Babylon: See Isaiah 21:9. Once a beautifully dressed woman (see Revelation 17:4), Babylon (Rome) became a deserted place for demons and unclean birds like vultures (compare Isaiah 13:20–22; Jeremiah 4:23–31; 50:39).

title: Revelation 18:3
content_html: Rome took wealth from conquered nations, living in extreme luxury, before facing God's justice.

title: Revelation 18:4
content_html: you will not share in her sins: Being connected to the evil city might result in sharing its punishment and even engaging in its sins (see Genesis 13:8–13; 19:1–29; compare Jeremiah 51:6, 45).

title: Revelation 18:4–8
content_html: This second poetic response (see study note on Revelation 18:1–24) warns from heaven to escape the doomed city.

title: Revelation 18:5
content_html: God has remembered: His response may seem slow (Psalm 103:8; 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2), but God is not weak. He does not forget good or evil (Revelation 16:19; Genesis 19:29; 1 Samuel 1:19).

title: Revelation 18:6
content_html: pay her back double: Some sins were so severe that they required double repayment (see Exodus 22:4, 7, 9; compare Isaiah 40:2; Jeremiah 16:18; 17:18).

title: Revelation 18:7
content_html: I sit as queen: Compare Ezekiel 28:1–2.

title: Revelation 18:8
content_html: The plagues (Revelation 18:2–3) remind us that destruction was not just a human action. The Lord God judges her. He is mighty and able to do what he promises.

title: Revelation 18:9–10
content_html: The kings of the world mourn the loss of the luxury they gained from their alliance with the wicked city.

title: Revelation 18:9–19
content_html: These three laments show the sadness of those who gained the most from the sinful city's wealthy lifestyle (compare Ezekiel 27).

title: Revelation 18:10
content_html: The kings, feeling scared and abandoned (see Revelation 18:15, 17), stand far away, trying in vain to escape punishment.

title: Revelation 18:11–17
content_html: The merchants of the world sing the second lament (Revelation 18:14; see study note on Revelation 18:9–19). They made money from the Roman economy (Revelation 18:12–13), but their trade stopped when the great city was quickly destroyed.

title: Revelation 18:12–13
content_html: This cargo list shows how much wealth Rome took from its empire (compare Ezekiel 27:12–24). It mentions some of the most expensive products of the ancient world, criticizing excessive materialism and pride.

title: Revelation 18:13
content_html: bodies: Rome had a large slave market, with historians estimating slaves made up nearly 20% of the population.

title: Revelation 18:14–17
content_html: The merchants would mourn the fall of Rome (see study note on Revelation 18:1–3) because it would permanently end their usual luxuries. Material goods can disappear in an instant.

title: Revelation 18:17–19
content_html: every shipmaster: Seafarers sing the third lament (see study note on Revelation 18:9–19). They mourn the breakdown of communication and transportation. During Julius Caesar's reign, the Mediterranean was cleared of pirates, and trade flourished under Roman law enforcement. The quick loss of Roman authority would disrupt the trade system.

title: Revelation 18:20
content_html: The laments (Revelation 18:9–19) prompt a call for heaven to rejoice. The people of God should not grieve because the judgment was for them. They had suffered persecution from the evil forces symbolized by the great city.

title: Revelation 18:21
content_html: a stone the size of a great millstone: People used heavy millstones, shaped for grinding olives or grain, throughout the ancient world. Jesus also mentioned using such stones for judgment (see Matthew 18:6). The dramatically thrown boulder symbolized that Rome's power was permanently destroyed.

title: Revelation 18:21–24
content_html: The songs about Babylon's fall (Revelation 18:1–24) end with a description of the city's destruction.

title: Revelation 18:23
content_html: Compare Jeremiah 25:10–11.

title: Revelation 19:1–2
content_html: This scene emphasizes what John heard instead of what he saw. A large crowd offers the first three-part praise (compare Revelation 7:9–10).

title: Revelation 19:1–10
content_html:
This section expands on the message of the sixth song of response (Revelation 18:20; see study note on Revelation 18:1–24), which called for rejoicing. Various groups offer praises to the Lord. The praises can be divided into two sections:

  • thankfulness for the destruction of evildoers (Revelation 19:1–4) and

  • thankfulness for the reward of God’s people (19:5–8).

title: Revelation 19:2
content_html: true and just (see Revelation 15:3; 16:7): In his righteous justice, God fulfilled his promise to judge the great prostitute, who symbolizes moral and spiritual corruption and the persecution of God's people.

title: Revelation 19:3
content_html: Her smoke: God's people praise him again when they see the fall of the evil city (compare Revelation 14:11).

title: Revelation 19:4
content_html:
• In response to the first two praises (Revelation 19:1–3), the elders and the living beings (see Revelation 4) again bow before God on the throne (see Revelation 4:10; 5:8, 14; 7:11).

  • Amen: See study note on Revelation 5:14.

title: Revelation 19:6–8
content_html: The final loud Hallelujah emphasizes that God rules as the all-powerful being (see Revelation 1:8; 4:8; 11:17; 15:3; 16:7, 14; 19:15; 21:22).

title: Revelation 19:7
content_html: For the marriage of the Lamb: This event, the wedding of the Messiah with his bride, the church (see Isaiah 54:5; 61:10; Jeremiah 31:32; Ezekiel 16:7–14; Hosea 2:16–20; Mark 2:19–20; 2 Corinthians 11:2), symbolizes complete victory and eternal fellowship.

title: Revelation 19:8
content_html:
• fine linen: See study note on Revelation 15:5–6.

  • the righteous acts of the saints: See Ephesians 2:8–10; 2 Timothy 3:16–17; James 2:18–22.

title: Revelation 19:9
content_html:
• Blessed are those: This fourth blessing in Revelation (see Revelation 1:3; 14:13; 16:15) confirms the hope of the faithful.

  • who are invited: God decides who will join.

  • marriage supper (compare Revelation 19:17): Jesus often used meals to explain the kingdom (Matthew 22:1–13; Luke 14:7–24), and he established a meal for the church (Mark 14:22–25; 1 Corinthians 11:23–26).

  • These are true words: This promise guarantees the message is trustworthy.

title: Revelation 19:10
content_html: The angel gives a strong warning against worshiping the wrong things (compare Revelation 22:8–9).

title: Revelation 19:11
content_html: The rider serves as both a judge and a righteous warrior (see Isaiah 11:1–5). He is called Faithful and True, representing God's authenticity and reliability (see Revelation 19:2; 21:5–6).

title: Revelation 19:11–16
content_html: Then I saw: John describes a new vision of Jesus Christ as a holy warrior and conquering king (see Revelation 14:1; see also Exodus 15:1–7; Psalms 24:8; 78:49–50; Isaiah 59:16–17).

title: Revelation 19:11–20.15
content_html: The drama reaches its peak: God's enemies face defeat and punishment in two episodes (Revelation 19:11–21; 20:7–10). Meanwhile, the faithful enjoy a 1,000-year resurrection (Revelation 20:1–6) followed by the final judgment (20:11–15).

title: Revelation 19:12
content_html:
• He has eyes like blazing fire: See Revelation 1:14–16; Daniel 10:6.

  • Christ, wearing many crowns, contrasts with the dragon, whose seven heads each had a crown (see Revelation 12:3).

title: Revelation 19:13
content_html:
• He is dressed in a robe dipped in blood: This may refer to:

    1. the blood of Christ’s enemies, showing his complete victory (Isaiah 63:2–4); or

    2. Christ’s sacrificial death for humanity (Revelation 1:7).

  • The Word of God is John's unique name for Jesus (see John 1:1, 14).

title: Revelation 19:14
content_html:
Christ’s heavenly armies, dressed in victorious white and riding on white horses, contrast with:

  • the locust forces from the abyss (Revelation 9:3–11),

  • the three frog-like evil spirits (Revelation 16:13), and

  • the defeated armies at Armageddon (Revelation 16:16; see also 19:19).

title: Revelation 19:15
content_html:
• from His mouth … a sharp sword: See Revelation 1:16; Hebrews 4:12; see also Isaiah 49:2; 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

  • The iron rod symbolizes Christ’s power as ruler and supreme shepherd (see Psalms 2:9; 23:4).

  • God the Almighty: See Revelation 1:8; 4:8; 11:17; 16:7, 14; 21:22.

  • His fierce wrath will crush his enemies like grapes in a winepress (Isaiah 63:2–4).

title: Revelation 19:16
content_html: KING … LORD: See Revelation 1:5; 15:3; 17:14.

title: Revelation 19:17–19
content_html:
gather together for the great supper: This feast on the flesh of the armies fighting against Christ contrasts with the marriage of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7). The enemies ready for battle are quickly destroyed (Revelation 19:20–21; see also 14:17–20; 16:16–21). Two feasts (the marriage supper of the Lamb in Revelation 19:7–8 and the "great supper" of God's judgment in Revelation 19:17–18, 21) offer two views on the end of time. They show the two sides of the good news:

  • grace and judgment, and

  • reward and punishment (compare John 3:16–18).

title: Revelation 19:20
content_html:
• the beast … the false prophet: See Revelation 13:1–10.

  • The fiery lake of burning sulfur symbolizes eternal punishment (see Revelation 20:10, 14–15; 21:8; see also Isaiah 66:24; Matthew 13:41, 49–50; Mark 9:43, 48).

  • God’s enemies are thrown into the fiery lake. The two beasts (Revelation 19:20) are followed by the dragon (20:10) and then by death (20:14) and unsaved humans (20:15).

title: Revelation 19:21
content_html: The entire enemy army is defeated by the sword from Christ’s mouth (see Revelation 1:16; 2:12, 16; see also Isaiah 11:4; 49:2; 2 Thessalonians 2:8). One side of God’s word (grace) leads to repentance, while the other side (judgment) delivers the death sentence.

title: Revelation 20:1–2
content_html:
Like the Old Testament angel of the LORD (see Genesis 16:7–11; 22:15; Exodus 3:2; Numbers 22:22–34; Judges 2:1–4; 6:11–22), the angel coming down from heaven has God's authority. This is symbolized by the key that controls the bottomless pit and the power to chain God's great enemy (see Revelation 12:7–9) for a thousand years.

See the "The Thousand Years (The Millennium)" Theme Note.

title: Revelation 20:1–10
content_html:
This passage has three parts:

  • the binding of Satan (Revelation 20:1–3),

  • the reign of God’s holy people (Revelation 20:4–6), and

  • Satan's release for a final battle attempt (Revelation 20:7–10).

Four themes emerge:

  1. Satan's war is pointless—he cannot resist even an angel;

  2. God’s holy people will be justified and honored;

  3. God will achieve the final victory; and

  4. even when sinful people experience Christ’s good purposes, they still follow Satan when he gains some freedom to act.

title: Revelation 20:3
content_html:
• This imprisonment, described as a bottomless pit (Abyss), represents an initial defeat for Satan and the forces of evil. Various New Testament passages explain how Christ's work impacts Satan (Luke 10:17–20; Acts 10:38; 26:18; Romans 16:20; Ephesians 6:11; James 4:7; see Matthew 12:24; 16:19; 18:18; John 12:31; 1 John 3:8). Christ's presence with his followers restrains evil powers (see 2 Thessalonians 2:7), reminding persecuted Christians that they have a future with God.

  • he could not deceive: Deception is the devil's hallmark, as he is a liar (see John 8:44; 1 John 3:8); no liar will enter heaven (Revelation 21:8, 27; 22:15). Faithful believers who experience Christ's reign will not be deceived.

  • The brief period of time might symbolize the three and a half years (see Revelation 11:2–3, 9–11; 12:14; 13:5)—it is a limited time.

title: Revelation 20:4
content_html:
• thrones, and those seated on them: This image of a heavenly court includes the elders (Revelation 4:4) on thrones (see Daniel 7:9–10), the victorious martyrs (see Daniel 7:22), and all of God's people.

  • beheaded for … Jesus: Beheading was a common execution method for Roman citizens.

  • The faithful who refused the mark of the beast (Revelation 13:16–17) will judge the condemned (see 1 Corinthians 6:2–3) on thrones like those of the worshiping elders (see Revelation 4:10–11; 5:8–10; 11:16).

title: Revelation 20:5–6
content_html: John compares the first resurrection with the second death (see Revelation 20:12–15; compare John 5:29).

title: Revelation 20:6
content_html: Blessed: This fifth blessing in Revelation (see also Revelation 1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 22:7, 14) highlights the promised reward of life. God's faithful people (Revelation 20:4) will reign with Christ as priests of God (see also 1:6; 5:10).

title: Revelation 20:7–8
content_html: When Satan is free to act (Revelation 20:7), he still tries to deceive. He will gather a large army (compare Ezekiel 38–39) in a final attempt to defeat God's people (Revelation 20:9).

title: Revelation 20:7–10
content_html:
Satan's attempt to defeat God's people ends with his fiery destruction. This passage has two main purposes:

  1. to demonstrate God's complete justice, as Satan and his followers deserve punishment and will not change; and

  2. to highlight the corruption of those who follow Satan. Even after witnessing Christ's rule for many years, they still join Satan when he is released.

These reasons form the basis for the judgment at the great white throne (Revelation 20:11–15).

title: Revelation 20:8
content_html:
• Jewish traditions differ on where Gog and Magog are located. Symbolically, they are to the north (see Jeremiah 1:14–15), the direction from which enemies traditionally approached Megiddo and Jerusalem. The names represent nations and rulers from all over the earth that oppose God's people (see Ezekiel 38–39).

  • to assemble them for battle: This repeats earlier scenes where God's enemies gather for battle (Revelation 16:16; 19:19). Here, Satan makes a final attempt to confront God and his forces.

title: Revelation 20:9
content_html: No battle happens (see Revelation 16:16–21; 19:19–21) because fire from heaven comes down and consumes them, just like in Sodom (Genesis 19:24; see also 2 Kings 1:10–12; Ezekiel 38:22–23; 39:6). God's enemies cannot stand against him.

title: Revelation 20:10
content_html:
They will be tormented … forever: Rebellion against God has eternal consequences. Some find the idea of endless punishment disturbing, but Revelation and Scripture generally convey that God will deal strictly with sin and rebellion by those who reject Christ's saving work (see Matthew 13:42; 25:41–46; John 3:18, 36; Romans 2:7).

More than any other book in Scripture, Revelation shows that the unrepentant continue their rebellion despite the consequences (Revelation 9:20–21; 16:9–11; compare Luke 16:31). Revelation also highlights that God is just and deals with sin as it deserves.

title: Revelation 20:11–15
content_html: This segment describes a court hearing where the dead are judged based on their actions. God responds to ongoing rebellion with eternal punishment.

title: Revelation 20:12
content_html:
• books were opened: See Psalm 56:8; Daniel 7:10.

  • The Book of Life contains the names of faithful people who have experienced God's saving power (Malachi 3:16; see Revelation 3:5; 13:8; 17:8; 20:15). They are likely the same ones who experience the first resurrection (Revelation 20:5–6).

  • the dead were judged: The works of both the redeemed and the unredeemed will be judged (see Revelation 22:12; Matthew 16:27; 25:31–46).

title: Revelation 20:13
content_html: Hades: See study note on Revelation 6:7–8.

title: Revelation 20:14
content_html:
• The phrase Death and Hades refers to the reality of death, which is the last enemy God will destroy (see Revelation 21:4; 1 Corinthians 15:26).

  • The lake of fire represents the terrible end for God's enemies who will not experience the first resurrection (see Revelation 20:5–6; compare Matthew 25:41; Luke 16:24; Jude 1:7).

title: Revelation 21:1
content_html:
• The vision describes God's creation of a new heaven and earth (compare Genesis 1:1 with Isaiah 65:17; 66:22). The old creation has vanished because it was subject to decay (see Romans 8:20–21; 2 Peter 3:7, 10).

  • the sea was no more: The sea symbolized chaos or the abyss (see study note on Revelation 9:1–12) and was sometimes depicted as a roaring monster (see Psalms 46:3; 74:14; see also Job 3:8; 41:1–34; Luke 21:25). The new creation will be free from all such evil.

title: Revelation 21:1–22.9
content_html:
The final scenes of the new heaven and earth show a new community and home for God's people, created and given by God. These scenes uplift persecuted Christians. John shares his main vision (Revelation 21:1–8) and expands on it in two parts:

  1. In Revelation 21:9–27, he describes the new Jerusalem as a place of holy perfection;

  2. in Revelation 22:1–5, he portrays the new Jerusalem as the ultimate garden of Eden, which Adam and Eve could have experienced if they had not sinned.

title: Revelation 21:2
content_html:
• coming down … from God: The new creation is a gift from God. At first, God created everything good. In the end, God will create a new, perfect world.

  • This image of Jerusalem as a beautiful bride (see Isaiah 61:10) contrasts sharply with the vision of Babylon (or Rome) as the prostitute (Revelation 17:4–5; 18:2–3; see study note on Revelation 21:9–22:9).

title: Revelation 21:3
content_html: The dwelling place of God is with man: The hopes of the Old and New Testaments are fully realized. Since humanity sinned (Genesis 3), separation from God has been humanity's biggest problem (Genesis 3:23). God has called his people to him through the prophets, Jesus his Son, and the Spirit's presence (see Genesis 17:8; Exodus 29:45–46; Psalms 46:4–5; 95:7; Jeremiah 7:23; 31:33; Ezekiel 34:14; 37:27; Zechariah 8:8; John 14:2–3; 17:24). In the new heaven and earth, God's people will finally enjoy ultimate and everlasting fellowship with God.

title: Revelation 21:4
content_html: no more death or mourning or crying or pain: See Isaiah 25:8; 1 Corinthians 15:54. God's people will experience the complete healing and salvation that Christ brings (see 2 Corinthians 5:17; Galatians 6:15).

title: Revelation 21:6
content_html:
• God repeats Jesus’s final words from the cross, It is done! (John 19:30; see also Revelation 16:17), linking Christ’s death to the promise of eternal life.

  • I am the Alpha and the Omega: Using the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, John shows God as the source of all reality and truth. God existed from the Beginning (see Genesis 1:1; see also John 1:1–2) and is also the End (see Revelation 1:8; 22:13).  

  • Since Christ is supreme and controls everything (see Romans 9:5; 1 Corinthians 15:28; Ephesians 1:22–23; 4:5–6), he provides the water of life to all who seek it (see study note on Revelation 7:16–17; see also Isaiah 55:1; John 4:10; 7:37–38).

title: Revelation 21:7
content_html: The one who overcomes: God's children who persevere in life will receive his blessings. Adoption is a covenant relationship. The language of adoption confirms their privileges and responsibilities (compare Genesis 17:8; 2 Samuel 7:14–16; Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 34:24).

title: Revelation 21:8
content_html:
• This list summarizes sins described in Revelation. These sins mark those not adopted by God; their fate is the fiery lake (see Romans 1:29–32; Galatians 5:19–21; 2 Timothy 3:2–5; compare Exodus 20:13–17; see also study note on Revelation 20:10).

  • all liars: John saw deception as the root of sin; he describes Satan as the father of lies elsewhere (John 8:44; 1 John 2:4; see Revelation 14:5; 21:27; 22:15; compare Matthew 12:34).

title: Revelation 21:9–22.9
content_html: This symbolic vision of the new Jerusalem uses vivid imagery to describe the bride, the wife of the Lamb. It represents all who accept Christ's message of salvation (see Revelation 21:2; 22:17; Ephesians 5:22–32).

title: Revelation 21:10
content_html:
• a mountain great and high: In Scripture, people often experience God on mountains (compare Ezekiel 40:2; see also Exodus 3:1; 19:10–25; Deuteronomy 34:1–4; 1 Kings 18:20–40; 19:8–18; Matthew 5:1; 15:29; 17:1; 24:3; 28:16).

  • the holy city … coming down out of heaven from God: People cannot reach God's presence and eternal life by their own efforts. They receive it as a gift (Ephesians 2:8–9; compare 2 Corinthians 5:1).

title: Revelation 21:11
content_html:
• Like God's true people, the city's glory reflects God's glory (compare Exodus 34:29–35; 2 Corinthians 3:7–11).

  • On earth, jasper is not transparent. But in heaven, the jasper is clear like crystal. Its perfect transparency shows God’s purity and integrity (see also Revelation 4:3; 21:11, 18).

title: Revelation 21:12–14
content_html: In this vision, the twelve tribes of Israel symbolize God's redeemed people (see Revelation 7:4–8; see also Romans 9:6–7). The twelve apostles of the Lamb form the foundation for this community of God's redeemed. Through Jesus, God has fully fulfilled his promise to bless all the families of the earth through Abraham (Genesis 12:3).

title: Revelation 21:15–17
content_html: measure the city: Measuring sets accepted boundaries. This contrasts with the unmeasured section of the temple (see Revelation 11:2 and Ezekiel 40:3–42:20).

title: Revelation 21:16
content_html: The city is a perfect cube. Each side measures 2253 kilometers (Greek 12,000 stadia). The number 12,000 symbolically represents the people of God (see “Symbolic Numbers” Theme Note).

title: Revelation 21:17
content_html: In the ancient world, walls were important for a city's defense, boundaries, and status. These walls, 65.8 meters thick (Greek 144 cubits), show the strength of God's redeemed people. The measurement of 144 cubits uses a multiple of 12 to symbolize God's people. A cubit was the length of a man's forearm, typically about 45.8 centimeters.

title: Revelation 21:18
content_html: The wall is made of jasper (see study note on Revelation 21:11; compare Revelation 4:3). The city is crafted from gold, which is not opaque like earthly gold. Instead, like God's transformed people, the heavenly gold is clear and pure.

title: Revelation 21:19
content_html: The twelve precious stones on the city's wall foundation remind us of the twelve stones on the high priest's breastplate. These stones represented God's people (see Exodus 28:17–20).

title: Revelation 21:21
content_html: In heaven, common building materials are the things we consider precious and luxurious on earth.

title: Revelation 21:22
content_html: I saw no temple: This seems to conflict with earlier visions (Revelation 11:19; 14:15–17). However, each vision in Revelation symbolically represents God's presence. There is no need for a temple in heaven because God is with his people.

title: Revelation 21:23
content_html:
• The city's vitality, energy, and life come from the glory of God and the Lamb, not from institutions or physical sources of power and light. The sun and moon were part of the first creation (Genesis 1:14–19; see Isaiah 60:19–20).

  • The Lamb is its lamp: Jesus is the light of the world (see John 8:12; 9:5; 12:35–36).

title: Revelation 21:24–25
content_html:
• the nations: God’s promise to Abraham comes true (Genesis 12:3; see Isaiah 2:3).

  • In Revelation, the kings of the world often represent God’s enemies (Revelation 6:15; 17:18; 18:3, 9). Now that these enemies are destroyed (Revelation 16:9, 11; 19:1–2, 19–21), the image is of God’s glory shown in those who have repented (Revelation 14:6–7; compare 11:13) and walk with God (5:9; 15:4).

  • The gates that are never closed contrast with ancient times when closed gates protected cities from enemies, especially at night. The people experience peace and security under God’s rule (see Isaiah 60:11). God’s enemies are gone, and fear and failure have ended (see Zechariah 14:7; John 11:8–10; 12:35–36).

title: Revelation 21:27
content_html: unclean: Spiritual impurity is a key concern in Revelation (see Revelation 21:8). Here, evil includes idolatry and dishonesty (see Revelation 14:5; 21:8; 22:15). Those not listed in the Book of Life cannot enter the city of God. Only those with names in the Book of Life, the pure of heart (Matthew 5:8), will see God.

title: Revelation 22:1
content_html: See Revelation 7:17; John 4:10–15; compare with Exodus 17:1–7; Isaiah 55:1; Ezekiel 47:1–10; Zechariah 14:8.

title: Revelation 22:1–2
content_html: This part of John's vision of the city shows how God provides water and food for his people (compare Exodus 16:4, 22–24; Ezekiel 47:1–12; John 4:10; 6:32–35). God first created a "garden of delight" (Eden) for Adam and Eve, but they disobeyed him and lost it (Genesis 2–3). Now, Eden is remade and joined with the heavenly city as God's wonderful gift for his faithful people.

title: Revelation 22:2
content_html: After humans sinned, they lost access to the tree of life (see Genesis 3:22–24), but now it is freely available. The tree stands on each side of the river (compare Ezekiel 47:12), showing there is no wrong side of the river in heaven. The tree produces fresh fruit every month, demonstrating God's constant provision.

title: Revelation 22:3
content_html:
• No longer … any curse: Since the first sin (Genesis 3:14–19), humanity has faced a curse due to their rebellion against God (see 1 Corinthians 16:22). Now, rebellion, sin, and the curse are gone forever.

  • His servants will worship him: Revelation shows examples of true worship (see Revelation 4–5; 7:9–17).

title: Revelation 22:4
content_html: The fear of death and seeing God will disappear (see Genesis 16:13; 32:30; Judges 6:22; Isaiah 6:5). God's people will carry his name and joyfully see his face (see Matthew 5:8; 2 Corinthians 3:18; 1 John 3:2–3).

title: Revelation 22:5
content_html: They will rule with God forever (see Revelation 20:4; see also 5:10) in his bright light that removes night and the need for lamps (see Isaiah 60:19–20).

title: Revelation 22:6
content_html: Everything John records in Revelation is trustworthy and true because God, who has all authority (compare Matthew 28:18), sent the messenger. However, these visions are not easy to understand.

title: Revelation 22:6–21
content_html:
This epilogue to Revelation includes words from an angel (Revelation 22:6, 8–11) and Christ (22:7, 12–19). It ends with a plea for Christ’s return (Revelation 22:20) and a final blessing (22:21). The epilogue connects directly with the book's introduction (Revelation 1:1–11). It highlights key themes such as:

  • encouraging faithful perseverance,

  • warning those who do evil things,

  • affirming the truth of the prophetic message, and

  • restating the nearness of Christ’s return.

title: Revelation 22:7
content_html: Blessed: The sixth blessing in Revelation (see Revelation 1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:14) is for those who obey. Obedience is essential for God's blessing.

title: Revelation 22:8–9
content_html: The warning against false worship (see Revelation 19:10) is repeated when John falls at the angel's feet again. Only God deserves our worship (compare John the Baptist's relationship to Jesus; John 1:6–8, 26–27; 3:27–35).

title: Revelation 22:10
content_html: Do not seal up: The angel expands John's initial instruction to Write on a scroll (Revelation 1:11) and warns against limiting its communication. Although some things are sealed, God does not reveal everything (compare Revelation 10:4). What has been written must be shared so people understand the message's seriousness and that the time is near (see Revelation 1:1, 3; 22:6–7; compare Daniel 12:4).

title: Revelation 22:11
content_html: People who refuse to accept God's forgiveness will remain wicked (see Revelation 16:9). John reminds good people to keep their integrity. This verse warns wrongdoers and urges the righteous to understand the importance of Christ's return for their commitments and actions.

title: Revelation 22:12
content_html: Christ is coming soon to repay everyone, not just based on their faith, but according to their actions (see Revelation 2:23; 11:18; 14:13; 20:12–13; see also Proverbs 24:12; Matthew 16:27; 2 Corinthians 5:10). Not every statement of belief is sincere (John 2:23–25); true faith shows through actions (James 2:14–26). We are saved by God's grace and power in Christ. Our actions do not save us, but they show the seriousness of our confession and provide a fair basis for our final reward or punishment.

title: Revelation 22:14
content_html:
• Blessed are those: In response to the link between actions and final outcomes (Revelation 22:12), the seventh blessing of Revelation (see also 1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:7) promises acceptance for those who wash their robes. These are people purified by trusting in Christ and following him faithfully (Revelation 3:5; 6:11; 7:9–10, 14; 19:13; Ephesians 5:26; Hebrews 10:22).

  • enter the city by its gates: See study note on Revelation 21:24–25.

title: Revelation 22:15
content_html:
• This verse lists those not allowed to enter, contrasting with those admitted in Revelation 22:14 (see 21:8, 27; 22:11). Unrepentant sinners stay outside the city. It warns that those who do not repent cannot enter the city of God (Revelation 22:14).

  • The term dogs for sinners was a common Jewish label for outsiders (see Matthew 7:6; Philippians 3:2; also 2 Samuel 3:8; 2 Kings 8:13); Jews used this term for Gentiles (see Mark 7:26–28).

title: Revelation 22:16
content_html:
• I am the Root and the Offspring of David: Jesus confirms the message of Revelation (compare John 21:24). He is the Root of David (see Revelation 5:5; Isaiah 11:10) and a descendant of David (see Ezekiel 37:23–24; Romans 1:3).

  • The morning star was a title for the Messiah (see Numbers 24:17; 2 Peter 1:19; compare Luke 1:78–79).

title: Revelation 22:17
content_html:
• Come is a repeated invitation and a confession (see Revelation 22:20).

  • the bride: The Lamb’s wife (see Revelation 21:9), represents the church, the people of God.

  • The thirsty can drink freely from God's provision (see Revelation 21:6; 22:1; Psalm 42:1; Isaiah 55:1; John 4:10–14).

title: Revelation 22:18–19
content_html:
• I testify: John swears to protect the integrity of the Book of Revelation. He curses anyone who changes the book's content or message (compare Deuteronomy 4:2; 12:32). This curse contrasts with the blessing for those who read aloud, listen to, and follow the prophecy (Revelation 1:3). When Revelation was written, scribes sometimes changed books to fit their views. Early Christians quickly developed ways to verify messages and messengers (see John 21:24; 1 Corinthians 16:21; Galatians 6:11; Colossians 4:18; 2 Thessalonians 3:17; 1 John 4:1–3).

  • Since the early church, the New Testament's scope and content have defined Christian teachings. However, some still try to change Scripture by adding works they consider equal to the Bible or by claiming parts of the Bible are unreliable. The true church has rejected and will continue to reject these attempts to change the canon, viewing them as human or even demonic efforts to alter the foundation of the Christian faith.

title: Revelation 22:20
content_html:
• I am coming soon: See Revelation 3:11.

  • Come, Lord Jesus!: Paul expressed the same wish (1 Corinthians 16:22; see also Philippians 4:5).

title: Revelation 22:21
content_html:
• Revelation ends with a blessing similar to those in Paul's letters (for example, Romans 15:33; 1 Corinthians 16:23–24).

  • The final sentence fittingly calls upon the grace of the Lord Jesus, which is the foundation of our forgiveness and the basis of our eternal hope.
